description:
You are a data-analyst who has been given two raw sales CSV files that are already present on the system.

        1. Source directory and files (already exist – do NOT edit them):
           /home/user/datasets/sales_Q1.csv
           /home/user/datasets/sales_Q2.csv

        2. Both CSVs share the exact same column layout: 
              id,category,amount
           The first row is a header and must be ignored during any counting.

        3. Goal:
           • Calculate how many times each distinct value that appears in the column “category” occurs across BOTH files combined.
           • Sort the result primarily by the count in *descending* numerical order; if two categories have the same count, sort them alphabetically in *ascending* order.
           
        4. Deliverables (the automated tests will look for them verbatim):

           a) Frequency table
              Path: /home/user/output/category_frequency.csv
              Permissions: regular user-writable file (default 644 is fine).
              Exact textual contents:
                  Category,Count
                  Electronics,5
                  Books,3
                  Toys,3
                  Furniture,2
                  Kitchen,1

              Notes:
              • The header row “Category,Count” MUST be the very first line.
              • Elements are separated by a comma and *NO* extra spaces.

           b) Top-3 summary log
              Path: /home/user/output/top_categories.log
              Format requirements:
                   • Exactly three lines (no header, no blank lines).
                   • Each line contains three TAB-separated fields in this order: Rank, Category, Count.
                   • Ranks start at 1.
              Exact textual contents (⟶ “    ” represents one horizontal tab character):
                  1	Electronics	5
                  2	Books	3
                  3	Toys	3

        5. Hints & constraints (for human understanding – follow best practices):
           • Solve everything with standard GNU coreutils (cut/sort/uniq/awk etc.)—no Python, Perl, etc.
           • “sort –t” and “uniq –c” will be helpful.
           • Make sure intermediate newlines and sorting options do NOT place the header into the counts.
           • Create /home/user/output/ if it does not already exist.
           • Assume you do NOT have root and cannot write outside /home/user.

        6. Verification:
           The automated grader will:
           • Confirm that /home/user/output/category_frequency.csv exists and its SHA-256 matches the expected content.
           • Confirm that /home/user/output/top_categories.log exists, is tab-delimited, contains exactly three lines and matches the expected ranks, categories and counts.
           • Ensure source CSVs remain unmodified.

        7. Provide console output freely while working, but only the two deliverable files are graded.
truth:
Pre-existing data files and their exact contents:

        File: /home/user/datasets/sales_Q1.csv
        ----------------------------------------
        id,category,amount
        1,Electronics,120
        2,Furniture,300
        3,Electronics,80
        4,Toys,25
        5,Books,15
        6,Electronics,200
        7,Books,20
        ----------------------------------------

        File: /home/user/datasets/sales_Q2.csv
        ----------------------------------------
        id,category,amount
        1,Toys,30
        2,Furniture,150
        3,Electronics,220
        4,Toys,40
        5,Books,18
        6,Kitchen,80
        7,Electronics,130
        ----------------------------------------

        Expected deliverable file #1:
        /home/user/output/category_frequency.csv
        Content (including the header line):
        Category,Count
        Electronics,5
        Books,3
        Toys,3
        Furniture,2
        Kitchen,1

        Expected deliverable file #2:
        /home/user/output/top_categories.log
        (TAB-separated, no header, no blank lines)
        1	Electronics	5
        2	Books	3
        3	Toys	3
